segments.s090-g25: همه سر به سر پاک is an idiomatic, intensive phrase: همه 'all' + the reduplicated سر به سر (an old poetic intensifier) + پاک used adverbially 'utterly/entirely'. در چنگ ماست is a locative phrase ('in the claw/grip of us') followed by the archaic copula ست (old form of است). Word order is emphatic: the quantified phrase comes first, then the locus 'در چنگ ما', and finally the copula.
segments.s090-g26: بر ایوانها is a prepositional locative ('on/upon the porticoes'). نقش و نیرنگ is a coordinated nominal predicate ('design and artifice/stratagem') followed by the possessive/agentive pronoun ما and the archaic copula ست. The sentence thus states location first and then names what is located there.
segments.s090-g27: This is an archaic/question order: interrogative کجا 'where' begins, followed by یافت (root 'find') and the future/modal auxiliary خواهی — so یافت + خواهی produces the meaning 'will find'. The pronoun تو is placed after the verb for emphasis, and آرامگاه is the object 'resting place/tomb'. In modern word order you'd expect آرامگاه را کجا خواهی یافت؟
segments.s090-g28: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereupon'. The question word کجا opens the clause and is followed by شد 'became' (simple past of شدن) and the predicate گرفتـار 'trapped/ensnared' with شاه 'the king' named after it; the verb-before-subject order is common in older poetic Persian.
segments.s090-g29: چنین داد پاسخ is 'he gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد past 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). The content clause is introduced by که. قارنم is a predicate nominal with the first-person enclitic -م ('I am [qāran]'); in older Persian copula may be absent and identity/predication is shown by the attached -م. Here قارن is used predicatively as 'equal/peer' (or possibly a name).
segments.s090-g30: گلیم اندر آب روان افگنم is an independent verbal clause where گلیم 'a kilim/carpet' is the object, اندر آب روان 'into the flowing water' is the locative phrase, and افگنم is a first‑person verbal form meaning 'I will cast/throw' (an archaic/poetic form of افکندن). The subject 'I' is omitted because it is supplied from the previous clause (من).
segments.s090-g31: The correlative negation نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...') negates two reasons: نه از بیم رفتم 'I did not go out of fear' — رفتم is simple past first‑person — and نه از گفت‌وگوی 'nor from/for talk/discussion'. The preposition از introduces the source/reason in both coordinated parts.
segments.s090-g32: به پیش پسرت آمدم is 'I came before/in the presence of your son' — به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the presence of', پسرت is 'your son' (possessed), and آمدم is past 'I came'. کینه جوی is an appositive/predicate noun 'seeker of vengeance' (کینه 'hatred/grudge' + جوی 'seeker' where جوی is used in the sense 'search/seeker'), describing the motive/role of the speaker.
segments.s090-g17: This is a poetic word order: the prepositional phrase 'ز گرد اندر' ('from among the throng') precedes the verb آمد (past 'came/appeared'); the noun phrase درفش سیاه ('black standard') is the logical subject and appears after the verb. 'اندر' is an archaic locative preposition meaning 'in/among'. 'درفش' (noun) is followed by the adjective 'سیاه'.
segments.s090-g18: 'سپهدار ترکان' is a noun phrase identifying whose standard it is (the sepahdar = commander, of the Turks). The following prepositional phrase 'به پیش سپاه' gives direction/place: 'to the front of the army'. Note that no new finite verb appears here; this phrase apposes or completes the preceding clause and specifies who/where.
segments.s090-g19: The verb برکشیدند (past, 3rd pl.) 'they raised/drew up' takes رده ('ranks') as its object; the adverbial phrase 'بر هر دو روی' ('on each of the two sides' = on both flanks) follows to specify where. 'هر دو' functions as 'both' (implicit 'دو' is understood with 'هر').
segments.s090-g20: برفتند is past, 3rd-person-plural, here meaning 'they advanced/proceeded'. The noun phrase گردان پرخاشجوی follows and is the group performing that action: 'the aggressive/combative troop(s)'. Poetic order places verb before its noun phrase.
segments.s090-g21: The prepositional phrase 'ز قلب سپه' ('from the heart/middle of the army') introduces the scene. 'ویسه' is the subject; 'آواز داد' is a light-verb construction meaning 'uttered a cry/raised his voice' (آواز = call/voice + داد = gave/emitted). Word order places the locative phrase before the subject and verb for emphasis.
segments.s090-g22: 'که' introduces the reported content ('that ...'). شد is the past of 'to become/happen' but here participates in the idiom 'به باد شد(ن)' meaning 'was blown away/ruined'. 'تاج و تخت بزرگی' places the qualifying noun 'بزرگی' (greatness) after the coordinated nouns 'تاج و تخت' — 'the great crown and throne'. 'به باد' is the literal 'to the wind' in the idiom.
segments.s090-g23: A geographic range introduced by the preposition 'ز' ('from') followed by the start-place 'قنوج' (Kannauj). 'تا' marks the extent 'as far as/to' and is followed by 'مرز کابلستان' ('the border/frontier of Kabulistan'). This is a standard 'from... to...' construction.
segments.s090-g24: 'همان' (= 'that/there') refers back to the preceding place and, with 'تا', continues the extent 'as far as'. 'در بست' functions here as a place-name or compound ('Darbast' / 'the closed gate'); in epic geography these two words form the next boundary mentioned. The conjunction 'و' then adds 'زابلستان' as the next region listed.
segments.s090-g33: چو (a temporal conjunction) = 'when'. از + کین او is a prepositional phrase 'from his hatred' (از = 'from/of', کین = 'hatred/vengeance', او = 'him'). دل is the direct object 'heart'. بپرداختم is a first-person singular past tense verb; here it functions idiomatically as 'I cast away / rid (my) heart of' his hatred (classical/poetic usage). Word order: temporal clause (چو ...) + prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s090-g34: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). کین و جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase 'hatred and war' serving as the object. ترا is the clitic object 'you' (ت = 2nd-person enclitic) plus the topic/object marker را; together they mark 'you' as the direct object of ساختم. ساختم is the simple past 'I made/caused' (ساختم = 'I made/raised'). Word order: temporal adverb + object phrase + clitic-marked object + verb.
segments.s090-g35: برآمد is the past tense 'arose/appeared'. چپ و راست = 'from left and right' (directions). گرد سیاه is a noun phrase 'black dust/cloud' (گرد here = 'dust' or dust-cloud, modified by سیاه = 'black'). The verb precedes these adverbial/directional phrases in poetic word order: 'arose left and right, a black dust'.
segments.s090-g36: نه ... نه is a correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. روی هوا literally 'the face/surface of the air' = 'the appearance of the sky'. ماند is 'remained/was left'. روشن = 'bright'. ماه = 'moon'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'neither did the face of the sky remain bright, nor (did) the moon (remain bright/visible)'.
segments.s090-g37: سپه = 'army/host' (subject). یک به دیگر is an idiomatic expression 'one upon another' / 'one against the other' (یک = 'one', به = preposition 'upon/to', دیگر = 'other/another'). برآویختند is the past plural verb 'they sprang upon/attacked'. The plural verb agrees with سپه (armies).
segments.s090-g38: چو = 'as/like' or 'when'; here it introduces a simile 'like a ...'. رود روان = 'a flowing river' (رود = 'river', روان = adjective 'flowing'). خون همی ریختند: خون = 'blood' (object), همی is a classical continuous/aspectual particle often marking ongoing action, and ریختند is the past plural 'they poured' (here 'poured blood'). Together: 'they poured blood like a flowing river.'
segments.s090-g39: بر ویسه = 'against Viseh' (بر + proper name). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/came' used here to introduce the action 'Qaren (subject) came against Viseh'. قارن is the agent/name (Qaren). رزم جوی is a compound/epithet 'rǝzm-jūy' = 'battle-seeking' or 'war-seeker' (رزم = 'battle', جوی = 'seeking'). The whole segment reads as 'Qaren, a war-seeker, came against Viseh.' Note the inverted poetic order: the prepositional phrase and auxiliary precede the subject.
segments.s090-g40: ازو = 'from him' (از + او), here referring back to the attacker. ویسه = 'Viseh' (subject of the action). در جنگ = 'in the battle' (در = 'in', جنگ = 'battle'). برگاشت (بـرگَشت) is a past tense verb 'he turned back/withdrew/turned away'. روی = 'face' used figuratively: 'he turned his face (away)'. Together: 'Viseh turned his face away from him in the battle' (i.e. withdrew/withdrew from the fight against him).
segments.s090-g9: This is a compact, slightly inverted Persian clause: ز ویسه ("from Viseh") gives the source, به قارن ("to Qaren") marks the indirect object, رسید is the past-tense verb "arrived/reached," and آگهی is the noun "news/information." The preposition ز before the source and the postposition به before the recipient follow classical poetic word order where the source precedes the recipient and the verb near the end.
segments.s090-g10: که introduces a reported content clause ("that …"). آمد is the simple past "came/arrived" used here idiomatically to mean "he came/returned" or "he appeared"; به + پیروزی expresses the manner/state ("in victory"), و links a parallel nominal attribute, فرهی, an epithet. The whole clause functions as the content of the news mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s090-g11: This noun phrase gives what was sent and where: ستوران is a collective noun for animals/stock (here rendered "herd/stock"), modified by تازی ("Arab/Arabian," here likely referring to Arabian mounts or Arabian stock), followed by سوی + نیمروز marking the direction/region "toward Nimruz." The order is Noun + modifier + direction, typical in Persian.
segments.s090-g12: فرستاد is the simple past of فرستادن "he sent." و connects the two coordinated actions. خود is the emphatic reflexive "he himself," and رفت is the simple past "went/left." گیتی فروز is a two‑word epithet (گیتی "world" + فروز "glorious/illuminating") attached to the subject, functioning like an honorific epithet after the verb. The clause therefore states a second action by the same subject.
segments.s090-g13: ز درد is a causal phrase "from/because of sorrow" (ز marking cause). پسر ویسه is a genitive/possessive noun phrase "the son of Viseh" and جنگجوی is a descriptive apposition "a warrior." The sequence places the reason first, then the person affected, following a poetic order that foregrounds cause.
segments.s090-g14: سوی پارس marks direction "toward Pars (Persia)." چون باد is a simile "like the wind." بنهاد is the past tense (classical/poetic) of نهادن with a prefixed b- (بنهاد = "he set/placed/put"), here used idiomatically with روی ("face") to mean "he turned/set his face." روی is the direct object "face/visage." The clause thus describes a swift turning toward Pars.
segments.s090-g15: چو here means "when" and introduces a temporal subordinate clause. از پارس indicates origin "from Pars," قارن is the subject (Qaren), به هامون marks the destination/direction "to the Hamun/plain," and کشید is the simple past of کشیدن "he drew/moved/advanced." The clause therefore situates a movement of Qaren in time and space.
segments.s090-g16: ز دست چپش is an ablative/locative phrase literally "from his left hand," idiomatically meaning "on his left flank/side." لشکر آمد پدید is a two‑word predicate: لشکر ("army") as subject, آمد (past "came") + پدید (an intransitive adjective/verb "appeared/manifested") meaning "appeared." The whole segment reports that an army showed up on his left.
segments.s090-g41: فراوان (fronted adverb) quantifies the clause: 'many' / 'in great number'. ز + جنگ آوران is a partitive/genitive expression 'of the warriors'; کشته شد is a passive past construction 'were killed' (کشته = 'killed', شد as passive auxiliary). Word order is adverb → partitive phrase → passive verb, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s090-g42: بآورد is past tense 'brought/carried' (آورد) with an implicit subject; it links this clause to the previous fact. چو ... شد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when/since' and ویسه سرگشته شد = 'Viseh became bewildered' (سرگشته = bewildered, شد = 'became'). Together: 'he brought (them) when Viseh became bewildered.'
segments.s090-g43: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر ویسه آمد is a verbal phrase 'came upon/to Viseh' (بر marks motion toward/upon the person). The subject 'اختر شکن' (the 'star‑breaker') is placed after the verb and introduced by ز — a poetic/inverted order: literally 'when upon Viseh came, from, the star‑breaker.'
segments.s090-g44: نرفت is the negative past 'did not go / did not leave'. از پسش is a prepositional phrase 'from after him / from his back'—idiomatically 'leave his side'. قارن رزم‌زن follows the verb as an appositive identification: 'Qarn, the warrior' (قارن = companion/associate here, but as a name; رزم‌زن is an epithet 'battle‑fighter').
segments.s090-g45: بشد here functions as a past/performative verb meaning roughly 'he proceeded / he went' or 'it came to pass'; it introduces the action of Viseh (ویسه) moving up to Afrasiab. تا پیش = 'up to / into the presence of' and افراسیاب is the addressee/goal.
segments.s090-g46: ز here is causal 'because of / from'. ز درد پسر = 'from grief/pain for (his) son' (درد = sorrow, پسر = son). مژه کرده پرآب is a resultative participial phrase: کرده (past participle 'made/caused') + پرآب ('full of water' / 'moist'), i.e. 'has made the eyelashes full of water' — idiomatically 'his eyelashes filled with tears.'
segments.s090-g1: بشد is a past 3rd‑person singular verb meaning 'he became/it came to be'. The proper name ویسه follows the verb (poetic inversion) as the subject. سالار is the predicative noun 'commander' and توران سپاه is a genitive group 'the army of Turan' (possessor + possessed compressed for poetic meter).
segments.s090-g2: ابا here functions in the sense of a poetic or contracted 'با' ('with' / 'having'), introducing the accompaniment; لشکری qualifies the military force (an adjectival/nominal form referring to troops), نامور is an epithet 'renowned', and کینه‌خواه is another epithet meaning 'vengeful' or 'seeking revenge'. (If read strictly from the provided lemma list, ابا can also mean 'to refuse', but in this line it serves as a prepositional/introductory element.)
segments.s090-g3: ازان پیشتر is a temporal phrase 'before that / earlier than that'. تابِه is the fused poetic sequence of تا + به ('until / as far as / up to'), followed by the proper name قارن (Qaren), and رسید is the past verb 'he arrived / he reached' — subject is omitted (3rd person).
segments.s090-g4: گرامیش is the affected person (here 'his dear / Gramish') and is marked by the direct‑object marker را. کشته is a past participle 'killed' and افگنده is a compound participle meaning 'thrown / cast down / laid low'; دید is the main past verb 'he saw'. The sequence means 'he saw (that) G. had been killed and cast down.'
segments.s090-g5: دلیران (plural) 'the brave ones / warriors' is coordinated with گردان (a troop or corps). توران سپاه forms the possessive group 'the corps of Turan's army' (possessor + possessed ordered compactly for verse).
segments.s090-g6: بسی is an intensive adverb 'many / much' (classical use), نیز 'also', با او 'with him'. فگنده is a past/participial form of افگندن used here in the sense 'set forth / were sent / marched' (poetic), and به راه 'on the road / took the road'. The subject ('they' / 'many') is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s090-g7: دریده 'torn' modifies درفش 'banner/standard' (adjective before the noun here for effect). نگونسار is an adjective 'mournful / woeful' modifying کوس 'drum' — the two adjective + noun pairs describe the battered, grief‑struck accoutrements of the army.
segments.s090-g8: چو ... چون is a pair of simile markers 'as/like ... like'. لاله 'tulip' and کفن 'shroud' with روی 'face' give a compact image: 'faces like tulips in a shroud' (i.e. vivid, shroud‑covered faces), and the second چون + سندروس compares them to sandalwood (sandalwood/scented wood); both are parallel poetic similes.

sentences.s090-s13: Everything is entirely in our grasp; upon the porticoes are our designs and artifices.
sentences.s090-s14: Where will you find a resting place? After that, where did the king become trapped?
sentences.s090-s15: He answered thus: 'I am an equal/peer.' / 'I am Qaran.'; 'I will cast the carpet into the flowing water.'
sentences.s090-s16: I did not go out of fear, nor out of talk; I came before your son as one seeking vengeance.
sentences.s090-s9: From the midst of the throng the black standard came forth — the Turks' commander — to the front of the host.
sentences.s090-s10: They drew up the ranks on both flanks, and the aggressive squadrons advanced.
sentences.s090-s11: From the heart of the host Viseh raised his voice, saying that the great crown and throne had been blown away.
sentences.s090-s12: From Kannauj to the frontier of Kabulistan, and thence as far as Darbast and Zabulistan.
sentences.s090-s17: When I cast his hatred out of my heart, now I have made hatred and war for you.
sentences.s090-s18: A black cloud of dust rose from left and right; neither the face of the sky remained bright nor the moon.
sentences.s090-s19: Armies sprang upon one another; like a flowing river they poured out blood.
sentences.s090-s20: Qaren, a war-seeker, set upon Viseh; Viseh turned away from him in the battle.
sentences.s090-s5: News from Viseh reached Qaren that he had come in victory and glory.
sentences.s090-s6: He sent Arabian stock toward Nimruz, and he himself departed, world‑glorious.
sentences.s090-s7: Out of sorrow, Viseh's son, a warrior, turned his face toward Pars like the wind.
sentences.s090-s8: When Qaren moved from Pars toward the Hamun, an army appeared on his left flank.
sentences.s090-s21: Many warriors were slain; he brought them when Viseh became bewildered.
sentences.s090-s22: When the star‑breaker came upon Viseh, the warrior Qarn did not leave his side.
sentences.s090-s23: Viseh went up before Afrasiab; from grief for his son his eyelashes were made full of water (i.e. filled with tears).
sentences.s090-s1: Viseh became the commander of Turan's army, with a renowned, revenge‑seeking soldiery.
sentences.s090-s2: Before that he went until he reached Qaren, and he saw his dear one slain and cast down.
sentences.s090-s3: Brave warriors and the Turanian corps set out — and many others also went with him on the road.
sentences.s090-s4: Their banner rent and the drum lamenting; their shroud‑covered faces were like tulips, like sandalwood.
